title: Determination of Viscosity of Liquids
aim: To determine the viscosity of a given unknown liquid (e.g., ethanol or glycol) relative to water using an Ostwald viscometer.
theory: Viscosity (η) is the internal friction of a liquid. Using an Ostwald viscometer, the time taken (t) for a fixed volume of liquid to flow through a capillary tube depends on its density (ρ) and viscosity. The relative viscosity is calculated using the formula: η2 / η1 = (ρ2 * t2) / (ρ1 * t1), where 1 refers to water and 2 refers to the unknown liquid.
procedure: Clean the viscometer thoroughly and fix it vertically to the retort stand.
Add a known volume (e.g., 20 mL) of distilled water into the wider bulb of the viscometer.
Using a suction bulb, pull the water up into the upper bulb until it is above mark 'A'.
Let the water flow down under gravity. Start the stopwatch when the meniscus passes mark 'A' and stop when it passes mark 'B'.
Repeat this process three times for water and find the average time (t1).
Empty and dry the viscometer, then repeat the same procedure for the unknown liquid to find average time (t2).
Measure the temperature using a thermometer to get density values (ρ1 and ρ2) from standard tables.
Calculate the relative viscosity (η2) using η1 (viscosity of water) at that temperature.

requiredFormula.description: η = Viscosity, ρ = Density, t = Flow time.

safety: Ostwald viscometer is extremely fragile; do not apply force while attaching the stand or bulb.
Do not suck liquids by mouth; always use a suction bulb.
Ethanol is flammable; keep away from open flames.
Avoid any debris in the viscometer as it can block the fine capillary tube.
